documents.title: Amp
documents.description: Amp is a frontier coding agent that lets you wield the full power of leading models.
documents.summary: This document introduces Amp, an agentic coding tool available for terminals and editors that leverages leading AI models for software development. It outlines product features, pricing, and recent announcements regarding its evolving capabilities.

documents.title: Amp Owner’s Manual
documents.summary: This document provides a comprehensive guide to installing and utilizing the Amp coding agent, covering its operational modes, prompting best practices, and configuration through AGENTS.md files.

documents.title: Models
documents.description: Amp uses the best model for each task: leading generalist foundation models for complex reasoning and planning, and smaller specialized models for fast, accurate responses in specific domains.
documents.summary: This document details the various foundation and specialized AI models Amp utilizes across different agent modes and system features to optimize performance for tasks like reasoning, search, and code review.

documents.title: Amp Free
documents.description: Free Usage, Built for Real Work. Amp gives you a $10 daily grant for all modes and models—funded by relevant dev and infra sponsors.
documents.summary: This document describes Amp Free, an ad-supported pricing tier that provides access to premium AI models and features through non-intrusive partnerships with developer tool companies.

documents.title: Chronicle
documents.summary: This document provides a chronological log of feature releases, model updates, and product changes for the Amp AI coding assistant platform.

documents.title: React server actions RCE reproduction case
documents.summary: This document identifies and describes the implementation of React Server Actions, covering server-side handling, client-side integration, and data serialization within the React codebase.

documents.title: Cool effect build review and files changed
documents.summary: Describes the technical implementation of synchronized visual effects between an animated orb and text components on a splash screen using shared state and custom rendering.

documents.title: Lua TUI framework design and widget sizing
documents.summary: This document explores architectural design choices for a Lua TUI framework, specifically analyzing the division of responsibilities between Zig and Lua for layout, rendering, and mouse interaction.

documents.title: Add parallel course correction agent with forced tool call
documents.summary: This document outlines the design and technical research for implementing a course correction agent that monitors AI model outputs to ensure adherence to plans and project guidelines.

documents.title: Migrate Vegeta plot from Dygraphs to uPlot
documents.summary: This document contains the embedded JavaScript logic and data structures required to render performance visualization charts for Vegeta load testing results using the uPlot library.

documents.title: Refactor video detail component architecture
documents.summary: This document outlines a technical refactoring strategy to improve the maintainability and readability of a video detail page by extracting layout components and simplifying state logic with custom hooks.

documents.title: Implement eval collection and filtering mechanism
documents.summary: This document explores technical strategies for implementing a collect-only flag in an AI evaluation CLI to list available tests without performing expensive execution.

documents.title: Slashing Custom Commands
documents.description: Custom commands are gone. Use skills instead.
documents.summary: This document explains the transition from custom commands to agent skills and provides step-by-step instructions for migrating existing commands both automatically and manually.

documents.title: GitHub repository ui-dojo exploration
documents.summary: This document details the architecture and features of UI Dojo, a reference project showcasing integrations between the Mastra AI orchestration framework and various AI UI libraries. It serves as a learning platform for developers to compare implementation strategies for agents, multi-step workflows, and generative user interfaces.

documents.title: Liberating Code Review
documents.description: Amp's code review agent is now composable and extensible.
documents.summary: This document explains the decoupled Amp review agent's capabilities, including its various invocation methods and the implementation of custom review criteria using scoped Checks.

documents.title: Painter
documents.description: Generate and edit images with the painter tool
documents.summary: This document explains how to use Amp's painter tool to generate and edit images for design inspiration and visual asset creation.

documents.title: Go Deep
documents.description: A new agent mode in Amp: deep. It thinks for longer, plans more, and needs you less.
documents.summary: This document introduces Amp's autonomous 'deep' mode, which uses GPT-5.2-Codex to perform extensive codebase research and solve complex problems independently.

documents.title: Shareable Walkthroughs
documents.description: Generate interactive shareable annotated diagrams
documents.summary: This document introduces Shareable Walkthroughs, an interactive tool for creating and sharing annotated diagrams that explain codebases, database schemas, and library functionalities.

documents.title: Tab, Tab, Dead
documents.description: We're removing Amp Tab. It is not part of the future we see.
documents.summary: This document announces the discontinuation of Amp Tab and explains the company's shift toward a post-agentic era where AI agents write the majority of code. It includes details on the sunset timeline and recommendations for alternative code completion engines.

documents.title: Raising an Agent
documents.description: A podcast by Quinn Slack and Thorsten Ball
documents.summary: This document provides a user interface instruction directing a user to choose a specific media episode for playback.

documents.title: How to Build an Agent
documents.description: Building a fully functional, code-editing agent in less than 400 lines.
documents.summary: A tutorial on building a functional code-editing agent in Go using the Anthropic SDK, demonstrating chat loops and the fundamentals of AI tool use.

documents.title: Amp Terms
documents.summary: This document outlines the legal terms and conditions governing the use of Sourcegraph's Amp service, including licensing, data privacy, intellectual property rights, and liability limitations.

documents.title: Appendix
documents.summary: This document provides technical guidance on troubleshooting, UI customization, and the configuration of the Amp permission system and MCP registry.

documents.title: Switch to Amp
documents.summary: This document provides a comprehensive migration guide for users switching from Claude Code to the Amp coding agent, highlighting benefits, equivalent features, and configuration steps.

documents.title: Amp Press Kit
documents.description: Media resources and information about Amp
documents.summary: This document introduces Amp, an agentic programming tool and coding agent, explaining the company's mission to transform software development through advanced research and product-focused exploration.

documents.title: Handoff (No More Compaction)
documents.description: Move work into new threads without losing your built-up context
documents.summary: This document introduces Handoff, a feature in Amp that replaces thread compaction by allowing users to extract task-specific context and files into new threads. It explains the transition from lossy summarization to a more focused, task-oriented approach for managing agent context.

documents.title: Rush Mode
documents.description: A faster, cheaper way to use Amp for small, well-defined coding tasks.
documents.summary: This document introduces Amp's 'rush' mode, a faster and more cost-effective agent setting optimized for simple coding tasks and small bug fixes.

documents.title: Debugging thread edit tool JSON implementation
documents.summary: This document analyzes and refactors a debug tool for editing thread data in JSON, focusing on synchronizing manual file changes with the application's state and UI.

documents.title: Optimize observeThreadGuidanceFiles return type
documents.summary: This document displays the execution results of a comprehensive test suite for the Sourcegraph AMP core library using the Vitest framework. It lists passed and skipped tests across various modules including filesystem tools, shell command parsers, and inference backends.

documents.title: Large Mode
documents.description: For the rare tasks that need up to 1M tokens of context.
documents.summary: This document introduces Amp's new 'large' agent mode featuring a 1-million-token context window and provides instructions on how to enable and use it effectively.

documents.title: SDK
documents.summary: This document provides an overview of the Amp SDK for TypeScript, explaining how to programmatically interact with the Amp agent through message streaming, thread management, and configuration settings.

documents.title: Context Management in Amp
documents.description: Learn how to get the most out of the context window in Amp, with the least amount of work.
documents.summary: This document explains the fundamental concept of the context window in large language models and provides instructions on how to manage context within the Amp agent platform using file mentions, shell mode, and editing tools.

documents.title: The Librarian
documents.description: A new subagent for searching code on GitHub
documents.summary: This document introduces the Librarian, an Amp subagent that allows users to search and analyze code across public and private GitHub repositories for debugging and implementation reference.

documents.title: Gemini 3 Flash in the Search Subagent
documents.description: The search subagent is now 3x faster with Gemini 3 Flash
documents.summary: This document announces the upgrade of Amp's codebase search subagent to Gemini 3 Flash, highlighting a 3x increase in speed and improved parallel tool call capabilities.

documents.title: Feedback Loopable
documents.description: How to make hard-to-debug problems debuggable by making them feedback loopable.
documents.summary: This document explains the concept of making problems feedback-loopable for AI agents by creating environments where they can independently validate their work and fix bugs.

documents.title: Amp Free
documents.description: Agentic coding is now free for everyone.
documents.summary: This announcement introduces Amp Free, an ad-supported tier of the Amp AI coding agent that provides access to agentic programming features and frontier models at no cost.

documents.title: Amp Security Reference
documents.summary: This document outlines the security architecture, compliance certifications, and data handling practices for the Amp AI coding agent to assist enterprises with risk assessments. It details how code data is processed by third-party providers and explains encryption, retention, and authentication standards.

documents.title: TODOs Are Done
documents.description: The TODO list feature is now gone from Amp
documents.summary: This document announces the removal of the TODOs feature from Amp, explaining that improved tracking capabilities in Opus 4.5 have made the feature obsolete and inefficient.

documents.title: Reassign database threads to Thorsten's user
documents.summary: This document outlines a plan to identify a specific user's ID and update thread ownership within a PostgreSQL database.

documents.title: User Invokable Skills
documents.description: Load Agent Skills when you decide, not the agent
documents.summary: Explains the new user-invokable skill feature in Amp, allowing users to manually force an agent to use a specific skill via the command palette.

documents.title: The Frontier Is Now Free
documents.description: $10/day for any mode, including smart with Opus 4.5
documents.summary: This document introduces the daily free credit system for Amp, detailing how users can access high-performance AI models through an ad-supported tier and manage their credit settings.

documents.title: Thread Map
documents.description: A birdseye view of the threads you forked, handed off, and referenced
documents.summary: This document introduces the Thread Map feature in the Amp CLI, which visualizes connections between related conversation threads and identifies common organizational patterns like hub-and-spokes and chains.

documents.title: Handoff, Please
documents.description: Move to a new thread without commands or buttons
documents.summary: This document describes a new feature that allows users to trigger agent handoffs using natural language commands to start new threads with context.

documents.title: Stick a Fork in It, It's Done
documents.description: We're ripping out the Fork command
documents.summary: This document announces the deprecation of the fork command in Amp and provides instructions on using handoff and thread mentions as more efficient alternatives for context sharing.

documents.title: Amp
documents.summary: This document presents user testimonials and a list of partner developer tools to showcase Amp's non-intrusive, developer-focused advertising model.

documents.title: Review
documents.description: A new panel for reading agent-generated code
documents.summary: This document introduces the Amp VS Code extension's new review panel designed to help developers efficiently review and manage code generated by AI agents.

documents.title: Amp Python SDK
documents.description: Amp Python SDK is now available
documents.summary: This document introduces the Amp Python SDK, explaining how to install and use it to programmatically execute Amp tasks and migrations from within a Python environment.

documents.title: Thread Labels
documents.description: Organize and find your threads with custom labels
documents.summary: This document explains how to use thread labels in Amp to organize and filter conversations for easier retrieval and better workflow management.

documents.title: Clickable Diagrams
documents.description: Mermaid diagrams now link directly to your code
documents.summary: Explains the new code reference feature in Amp-generated Mermaid diagrams that allows users to jump to specific code locations from diagram elements.

documents.title: Workspace Settings
documents.description: Configure the Amp CLI per workspace
documents.summary: This document explains how to use workspace-specific configuration files in the Amp CLI to manage project-level settings and MCP servers.

documents.title: Gemini 3 Pro
documents.description: Google's Gemini 3 is now the main agent model in Amp
documents.summary: This document announces that Gemini 3 Pro has become the default model for Amp's smart agent mode, detailing its performance improvements over previous models and outlining current known limitations.

documents.title: Workspace Entitlements
documents.description: Per-user usage quotas for Amp Enterprise Premium workspaces
documents.summary: This document explains how to configure and manage per-user spending limits through the workspace entitlements feature for Amp Enterprise Premium accounts.

documents.title: Social Coding
documents.description: See how other people are actually using coding agents to do real stuff, with public profiles and threads.
documents.summary: This document introduces public profiles and thread sharing on the Amp platform, allowing users to learn from real-world AI agent workflows and claim their own public usernames.

documents.title: Agentic Review
documents.description: A new agent for reviewing agent-generated code in your editor
documents.summary: This document introduces Amp's new specialized code review agent for VS Code, detailing its workflow for analyzing diffs and generating actionable feedback within an integrated development environment.

documents.title: GPT-5 Oracle
documents.description: When Sonnet falls short, GPT-5 can now help as the oracle
documents.summary: This document announces that GPT-5 has transitioned from an experimental feature to the permanent model for the oracle in Amp, highlighting its strengths in planning, debugging, and architectural analysis.

documents.title: Monthly Automatic Top Ups
documents.description: A new mode for a more predictable Amp budget
documents.summary: This document introduces the new monthly automatic top-up feature for Amp balances, which allows users to set a recurring monthly budget while retaining unused credits.

documents.title: Look At This
documents.description: Analyze PDFs and images without bloating your context window
documents.summary: This document explains how Amp uses the look_at tool to process large media files via a separate model, minimizing main context window usage by extracting only the requested data.

documents.title: Enterprise Managed Settings
documents.description: Configure organization-wide settings that override individual settings
documents.summary: This document explains how system administrators can implement organization-wide managed settings in Amp to enforce security and standardization across Enterprise accounts. It details the configurable parameters and provides the specific file paths required for different operating systems.

documents.title: Opus 4.5: Better, Faster, Often Cheaper
documents.description: Claude Opus 4.5 is the new main agent model in Amp
documents.summary: This document announces and explains the transition of Amp's 'smart' mode to the Claude Opus 4.5 model, providing a comparative analysis of its performance, reliability, and cost-efficiency against other models like Gemini 3 and Sonnet 4.5.

documents.title: Find Threads
documents.description: Find Amp threads by keyword or by which files they touched
documents.summary: This document introduces the find_thread tool for Amp, which enables users to search historical conversation threads using keywords or by identifying threads that modified specific files.

documents.title: Through the Agent, Into the Shell
documents.description: Execute shell commands, right there in the prompt input
documents.summary: This document introduces and explains the shell mode feature for the Amp CLI, which allows users to execute shell commands directly within the prompt and manage their inclusion in the agent context window.

documents.description: <!doctype html> <html lang="en"> <head> <meta charset="utf-8" /> <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1, maximum-scale=1, user-scalable=no" /> <link rel="manifest" href="/site.webmanifest" /> <link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/app-icon.png" /> <meta name="apple-mobile...
documents.summary: This document announces the transition of the standard agent instruction filename from AGENT.md to AGENTS.md to align with emerging industry standards set by OpenAI.

documents.title: 1,000,000 Tokens
documents.description: Amp can now use 1 million tokens of context with Claude Sonnet 4
documents.summary: This document announces Amp's expanded 1 million token context window for Claude Sonnet 4 and provides recommendations for optimizing performance and cost through small, task-specific threads.

documents.title: JetBrains Diagnostics for Java, Kotlin, and Scala
documents.description: Read JVM diagnostics from JetBrains IDEs
documents.summary: This document explains how Amp CLI integrates with JetBrains IDEs to retrieve diagnostics for JVM-based languages like Java, Kotlin, and Scala.

documents.title: Agent Skills
documents.description: Reuse your existing agent skills in Amp
documents.summary: This document introduces Amp agent skills, which allow agents to lazily load specific tool instructions, and outlines the directory structure for skill installation.

documents.title: Amp Now Reads Threads
documents.description: Reference and let Amp read other Amp threads
documents.summary: This document outlines how to use thread referencing in Amp to provide contextual information for AI agents through URLs, IDs, or the @ symbol. It details the process of using the read_thread tool to automatically extract relevant markdown from linked conversations.

documents.title: Command Palette, Not Slash Commands
documents.description: Amp now has a command palette that replaces slash commands
documents.summary: This document introduces the Amp Command Palette for the CLI and editor extensions, detailing its keyboard shortcuts and improvements over the previous slash menu.

documents.title: Model Evaluation
documents.description: Follow our team's real-time evaluation of new frontier models
documents.summary: This document chronicles the Amp team's evaluation of new large language models like GPT-5 and Claude Opus 4.1, detailing their practical performance in an agentic coding environment.

documents.title: Amp Tab 30% Faster
documents.description: Amp Tab response times are now 30% faster with optimized infrastructure
documents.summary: This document describes infrastructure optimizations and performance improvements to the Amp Tab in-editor completion engine, highlighting techniques like speculative decoding and TensorRT-LLM.

documents.title: Thread Forking in VS Code
documents.description: We added fork threads to help you explore different directions mid-thread
documents.summary: This document explains the functionality and benefits of the Fork feature, which allows users to create new conversation threads from a specific point.

documents.title: Amp, Inc.
documents.description: Amp is becoming its own company
documents.summary: This document announces that Amp is spinning off from Sourcegraph to become an independent research lab focused on leveraging artificial intelligence to transform software development.

documents.title: Amp TypeScript SDK
documents.summary: This document introduces the Amp TypeScript SDK, explaining how to programmatically integrate the Amp agent into TypeScript applications for automated tasks.

documents.title: Use Amp Free at Work
documents.description: The Internet’s best business model (advertising) delivers once again: a $0 coding agent that meets the same stringent infosec standards of Amp’s paid smart mode.
documents.summary: This document announces that Amp Free no longer requires model training and now adheres to the same security standards as the paid version. It provides instructions for users and administrators on how to enable and manage free mode in the CLI, editor, and workspace settings.

documents.title: MCP Permissions
documents.description: You can now set `amp.mcpPermissions` to define rules that block or allow MCP servers
documents.summary: This document explains how to configure rule-based permissions for Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers using the amp.mcpPermissions setting to allow or block specific commands and URLs.

documents.title: Try GPT-5 With Us
documents.description: Test GPT-5 in Amp while we evaluate its agentic capabilities
documents.summary: This document explains how to temporarily enable and test GPT-5 integration within the Amp CLI and editor extensions during the model's evaluation phase.

documents.title: Multiple AGENT.md Files
documents.description: Use multiple `AGENT.md` files across directory trees
documents.summary: This document explains how the Amp editor utilizes AGENTS.md files across various directory levels to provide localized context and configuration. It details the file search hierarchy and the ability to include additional file references within these documents.

documents.title: Diagnostic-Driven Completions
documents.description: Amp Tab now uses diagnostics to suggest file-wide fixes
documents.summary: This document explains how Amp Tab uses language server diagnostics to suggest follow-up edits and provide file-wide completions for resolving introduced errors.

documents.title: Terminal Improvements
documents.description: Commands now inherit shell environments and support interactive mode
documents.summary: This document outlines updates to terminal command execution in Amp, including shell environment integration, new interactive controls, and improved output rendering.

documents.title: Towards a New CLI
documents.description: Today we released a new version of our Amp CLI
documents.summary: This document announces the updated version of the Amp CLI, designed to integrate and support AI agents directly within the terminal environment. It highlights the growing trend of terminal-based agents and provides basic commands for installation and authentication.

documents.title: No More BYOK
documents.description: The best product is built by iterating fast at the model↔product frontier, so we removed Isolated Mode
documents.summary: This document explains the decision to remove Isolated Mode in Amp, detailing the technical challenges regarding API rate limits, connectivity, and model parity that led to the change.

documents.title: Edit, Restore and Fork in the CLI
documents.description: Edit, restore and fork previous messages in the CLI with Tab
documents.summary: This document explains how to manage message threads in the Amp CLI by using keyboard shortcuts to edit, restore, or fork messages.

documents.description: <!doctype html> <html lang="en"> <head> <meta charset="utf-8" /> <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1, maximum-scale=1, user-scalable=no" /> <link rel="manifest" href="/site.webmanifest" /> <link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/app-icon.png" /> <meta name="apple-mobile...
documents.summary: This document introduces AGENT.md and AGENTS.md files, which are used by the Amp tool to store project-specific guidance on structure, build steps, and coding conventions.

documents.title: 200k Tokens Is Plenty
documents.description: Short threads are cheaper, better, and easier to reason about.
documents.summary: This document explains the benefits of using short, task-specific threads instead of long context windows when working with AI coding agents to improve performance and reduce costs.

documents.title: Amp CLI connects to VS Code and Neovim
documents.description: Amp CLI connects to VS Code and Neovim
documents.summary: This document explains how to integrate the Amp CLI with VS Code and Neovim to enable direct file access and editing within the IDE.

documents.title: Efficient MCP Tool Loading
documents.description: Load MCP tools into your context window only when you use them
documents.summary: This document explains how to optimize token usage in Amp by integrating MCP server configurations with Agent Skills to load tool definitions dynamically only when a skill is invoked.

documents.description: <!doctype html> <html lang="en"> <head> <meta charset="utf-8" /> <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1, maximum-scale=1, user-scalable=no" /> <link rel="manifest" href="/site.webmanifest" /> <link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/app-icon.png" /> <meta name="apple-mobile...
documents.summary: This document explains how to use glob patterns in AGENTS.md files to dynamically include external documentation and context files for AI agents.

documents.title: Bring Your Own Tools
documents.description: Provide tools to Amp in the form of executable files
documents.summary: This document explains how to integrate custom executable files as tools in Amp using environment variables and standard input/output protocols for discovery and execution.

documents.title: Amp Now Uses Claude Sonnet 4
documents.description: Now that Anthropic has released it, we've switched Amp's primary model to be Claude Sonnet 4
documents.summary: This document announces Amp's transition to using Claude 4 Sonnet as its primary model and outlines the improvements in speed, stability, and tool calling capabilities.

documents.title: Multi-Root Workspaces
documents.summary: This document describes the improved support for multi-root workspaces in Amp, detailing updates to file management, UI visibility, and search capabilities.

documents.title: Agents Panel
documents.description: A new panel for viewing and managing multiple agent threads
documents.summary: This document introduces the Agents Panel in the Amp editor extension and explains how to navigate, manage, and archive agent threads using keyboard shortcuts.

documents.title: Episode 8
documents.description: In this episode, Beyang sits down with Camden to discuss how the Amp team evaluates new models: why tool calling is the key differentiator, how open models like K2 and Qwen stack up, what GPT-5 changes, and how qualitative "vibe checks" often matter more than benchmarks. They also dive into subagents, model alloys, and what the future of agentic coding looks like inside Amp.
documents.summary: This document explores how the Amp team evaluates large language models for agentic coding, specifically focusing on the importance of tool calling, model alloying, and the ability of agents to self-correct during complex tasks.

documents.title: Queue Messages in the CLI
documents.description: Queue messages with `/queue`
documents.summary: This document explains how to use CLI commands to queue and dequeue messages for an agent to process sequentially.

documents.title: Amp is now available. Here's how I use it.
documents.description: Amp is now available. Here is how I use it.
documents.summary: This document outlines a professional developer's workflow and best practices for using the Amp coding agent, covering feature implementation, visual debugging, and context management.

documents.title: How We Build
documents.description: How we build Amp
documents.summary: This document outlines the core engineering philosophy and cultural values of a development team, emphasizing shipping speed, individual accountability, and minimalist design standards.

documents.title: Oracle
documents.description: Meet Oracle, Amp's new o3-powered tool for code review and analysis
documents.summary: This document introduces the 'oracle' subagent for the Amp coding platform, which leverages the OpenAI o3 model to assist with complex code reviews, debugging, and architectural analysis.

documents.title: How We Think about Permissions
documents.summary: This document explains the Amp permission system and how to configure rules that control an AI agent's access to tools, files, and commands. It covers various scenarios ranging from full automation to granular restrictions for sensitive operations like database management and git commits.

documents.title: Frequently Ignored Feedback (FIF)
documents.description: Our responses to some common feedback that we are intentionally not acting on.
documents.summary: This document outlines the product philosophy for the Amp coding assistant by explaining why specific feature requests are intentionally declined. It provides context on the developers' decisions regarding model selection, agent autonomy, and user interface design.

documents.title: TODOs for the Agent
documents.description: The TODO feature that lets the agent manage its own list of tasks is now enabled by default
documents.summary: This document announces that the agent-managed task list (TODO) feature is now enabled by default.

documents.title: Episode 9
documents.description: In this episode, Quinn and Thorsten discuss how everything seems to have changed again with Gemini 3 and Opus 4.5 and what comes after — the assistant is dead, long live the factory.
documents.summary: This document explores the evolution of AI development from simple coding assistants to autonomous agent-driven factories capable of managing entire codebases independently. It analyzes how recent advancements in frontier models are shifting the industry toward a future where agents perform the majority of software engineering tasks.

documents.title: cje's Profile
documents.summary: This document displays a user profile for Christopher Ehrlich, showcasing developer metrics such as message counts, code contribution statistics, and preferred development tools.

documents.title: Now Using Claude Sonnet 4.5
documents.description: Anthropic released an updated Sonnet 4.5, and Amp now uses it by default
documents.summary: This document announces that Amp has transitioned to Claude Sonnet 4.5 as its primary model, detailing improvements in coding performance and update instructions for users.

documents.title: mitsuhiko's Profile
documents.summary: This document displays the developer profile and contribution statistics for Armin Ronacher, including metrics for messages, threads, and code changes.

documents.title: Amp Tab for All
documents.description: Our free in-editor completion engine for VS Code, Cursor and Windsurf is now on by default for new installs
documents.summary: This document introduces Amp Tab, a fast in-editor code completion engine for VS Code, Windsurf, and Cursor that supports multi-file edits and error resolution. It provides instructions for enabling the extension and highlights its key diagnostic and editing features.

documents.title: Agents for the Agent
documents.description: Amp now has new subagents and they're more powerful than ever. Is everything changing again?
documents.summary: This document explains the concept and implementation of subagents in Amp, detailing how they function as autonomous tools to handle specific tasks and manage context windows more efficiently. It explores the benefits of task delegation, such as parallel processing and reduced token consumption for the main agent.

documents.title: Update search agent eval dashboard reporting
documents.summary: This document is empty and contains no substantive information or instructions to analyze.

documents.title: Image Support in CLI
documents.description: Reference images by pasting paths or dragging files into the terminal
documents.summary: This document explains how to reference image files in the Amp CLI using file paths, drag-and-drop, or the @ symbol for file mentions to enhance prompting.

documents.title: Episode 3
documents.description: Quinn and Thorsten start by sharing how reviews are still very much needed when using AI to code and how it changes the overall flow you're in when coding with an agent. They also talk about a very important question they face: how important is code search, in its current form, in the age of AI agents?
documents.summary: This discussion explores how AI agents are transforming the software engineering workflow, shifting the developer's role from writing manual code to guiding AI drafts through high-level instructions and reviews.

documents.title: amp dash x
documents.description: The Amp CLI has a new flag: `--execute`
documents.summary: This document introduces the Amp CLI's execute mode, explaining how to use the --execute flag for programmatic interactions and automation via the command line.

documents.title: tsenart's Profile
documents.summary: This document displays a user profile for Tomás Senart on the ampcode platform, detailing developer activity metrics such as messages, threads, and code contributions.

documents.title: Streaming JSON
documents.description: The Amp CLI has two new flags: `--stream-json` and `--stream-json-input` and stuff
documents.summary: This document introduces the --stream-json and --stream-json-input flags for the Amp CLI, which allow for structured JSON input and output during command execution. It explains how to use these flags to programmatically interact with the CLI and maintain multi-turn conversations.

documents.title: marcel's Profile
documents.summary: This document presents a user profile for Marcel Fahle on the Ampcode platform, displaying developer activity metrics such as message counts, thread participation, and code contribution statistics.

documents.title: PostHog invitation entity ER diagram walkthrough
documents.summary: This document describes the entity relationship model for PostHog's invitation system, explaining how organizations, users, and memberships interact through the invitation flow.

documents.title: Episode 5
documents.description: In this episode, Beyang interviews Thorsten and Quinn to unpack what has happened in the world of Amp in the last five weeks: how predictions played out, how working with agents shaped how they write code, how agents are and will influence model development, and, of course, all the things that have been shipped in Amp.
documents.summary: A transcript of a discussion exploring the technical evolution of AI coding agents and the design philosophy behind the Amp project. It examines the relationship between large language models, tool-calling capabilities, and the practical challenges of building autonomous developer tools.

documents.title: Svelte 5 event handling improvements vs version 4
documents.summary: This document compares the evolution of event handling between Svelte 4 and Svelte 5, detailing the transition from directives and dispatchers to standard attributes and callback props.

documents.title: JetBrains in the CLI
documents.description: Connect Amp CLI to JetBrains IDEs for context and editing
documents.summary: This document explains how to integrate the Amp CLI with JetBrains IDEs to enable file context sharing and direct editing capabilities.

documents.title: 50% Faster Search Agent
documents.description: Search agent is now 50% faster with optimized parallel execution
documents.summary: This document announces an update to Amp's search subagent, which now utilizes the Haiku 4.5 model for improved speed and cost-efficiency. The update results in faster context discovery with a significant reduction in operational costs.

documents.title: Email invite acceptance process walkthrough
documents.summary: This document provides a technical walkthrough of the PostHog email invite acceptance process, detailing the sequence of events from initial validation to member onboarding and analytics reporting.

documents.title: Episode 4
documents.description: Thorsten and Quinn talk about the future of programming and whether code will still be as valuable in the future, how maybe the GitHub contribution graph is already worthless, how LLMs can free us from the tyranny of input boxes, and how conversations with an agent might be a better record of how a code change came to be than git commit tools. They also share where it works and simply doesn't work.
documents.summary: This transcript features a discussion between Sourcegraph leadership about the evolving value of source code and the shift in developer workflows as AI agents take a more central role in programming. It explores how automated generation changes the perception of code quality and the potential for agents to manage complex feedback loops and system architectures.

documents.title: Episode 2
documents.description: Thorsten and Quinn talk about how different agentic programming is from normal programming and how the mindset has to adapt to it. One thing they discuss is that having a higher-level architectural understanding is still very important, so that the agent can fill in the blanks. They also talk about how, surprisingly, the models are really, really good when they have inputs that a human would normally get. Most importantly, they share the realization that subscription-based pricing might make bad agentic products.
documents.summary: This document explores the shift from traditional to agentic programming, emphasizing how architectural intuition is critical for guiding AI agents through complex, non-local code changes.

documents.title: Episode 7
documents.description: In this episode, Beyang and Thorsten discuss strategies for effective agentic coding, including the 101 of how it's different from coding with chat LLMs, the key constraint of the context window, how and where subagents can help, and the new oracle subagent which combines multiple LLMs.
documents.summary: This transcript explores the transition from chat-based AI assistants to autonomous coding agents, detailing strategic approaches to managing subagents and tool-enabled workflows. It explains the importance of initial prompt specification and context window preservation in the modern agentic programming paradigm.

documents.title: Tool-Level Permissions
documents.description: Configure granular permissions for individual tools and arguments
documents.summary: This document explains how to configure granular tool-level permissions in Amp, allowing users to define specific rules for allowing, rejecting, or delegating tool calls and Bash commands.

documents.title: Add visual effect to splash screen welcome message
documents.summary: This document outlines the design and implementation plan for synchronizing a text-based illumination effect with an animated orb widget in a terminal user interface splash screen.

documents.title: Episode 6
documents.description: In this episode, Quinn and Thorsten discuss Claude 4, sub-agents, background agents, and they share "hot tips" for agentic coding.
documents.summary: This podcast transcript features a discussion on the evolving landscape of AI coding agents, focusing on the capabilities of Claude 3.5 Sonnet and the development of the Amp coding tool. The hosts explore technical concepts like background agents, the shift from manual coding to agentic workflows, and the design philosophies of leading AI model providers.

documents.title: Episode 1
documents.description: In the first episode of Raising an Agent, Quinn and Thorsten kick things off by sharing a lot of wow-moments they experienced after getting the agent at the heart of Amp into a working state. They talk about how little is actually needed to create an agent and how magical the results are when you give a model the right tools, unlimited tokens, and feedback. That might be the biggest surprise: how many previous assumptions feel outdated when you see an agent explore a codebase on its own.
documents.summary: This transcript introduces a podcast series about building an agentic AI code editor, exploring the development process and the surprising capabilities of tool-calling models in software engineering.

documents.title: A Codebase by an Agent for an Agent
documents.description: A Codebase by an Agent for an Agent
documents.summary: This document explores the concept of allowing AI agents to determine codebase structure and naming conventions to optimize their efficiency and performance. It argues that aligning code with an agent's probabilistic training data reduces cognitive friction and enables the agent to navigate and develop the framework more effectively.
